Part I: Morphological Data

Question 1

species.mean.table <- iris %>% group_by(Species) %>% summarize(across(where(is.numeric), mean))
print(species.mean.table)
## # A tibble: 3 × 5
##   Species    Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width
##   <fct>             <dbl>       <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl>
## 1 setosa             5.01        3.43         1.46       0.246
## 2 versicolor         5.94        2.77         4.26       1.33 
## 3 virginica          6.59        2.97         5.55       2.03

Question 2

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Petal Length", y = "Petal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Petal Width", y = "Petal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Petal Length", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Sepal Length", y = "Petal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Petal Length", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Petal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Sepal Width", y = "Petal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Petal Width", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Sepal Length", y = "Petal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Petal Width", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Sepal.Width", y = "Petal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Sepal Length", y = "Sepal Width") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Sepal Width", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_bw()

## Question 3 Sepal length and petal width give the best predictions of species because the LD1 and LD2 values when sepal length and pedal width are combined are the highest at 0.9999 and 0.001 respectively compared to other combinations. For example, sepal length and sepal width together give a lower correlation with LD1 and LD2 at 0.9628 and 0.0372 respectively.

data <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Petal.Length, Petal.Width)

group <- iris %>%
    pull(Species)

lda(data, group)
## Call:
## lda(data, group)
## 
## Prior probabilities of groups:
##     setosa versicolor  virginica 
##  0.3333333  0.3333333  0.3333333 
## 
## Group means:
##            Petal.Length Petal.Width
## setosa            1.462       0.246
## versicolor        4.260       1.326
## virginica         5.552       2.026
## 
## Coefficients of linear discriminants:
##                   LD1       LD2
## Petal.Length 1.544371 -2.161222
## Petal.Width  2.402394  5.042599
## 
## Proportion of trace:
##    LD1    LD2 
## 0.9947 0.0053
data <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Petal.Length, Sepal.Width)

group <- iris %>%
    pull(Species)

lda(data, group)
## Call:
## lda(data, group)
## 
## Prior probabilities of groups:
##     setosa versicolor  virginica 
##  0.3333333  0.3333333  0.3333333 
## 
## Group means:
##            Petal.Length Sepal.Width
## setosa            1.462       3.428
## versicolor        4.260       2.770
## virginica         5.552       2.974
## 
## Coefficients of linear discriminants:
##                    LD1       LD2
## Petal.Length  2.482128 0.3724063
## Sepal.Width  -1.625151 2.7330003
## 
## Proportion of trace:
##   LD1   LD2 
## 0.991 0.009
data <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Petal.Length, Sepal.Length)

group <- iris %>%
    pull(Species)

lda(data, group)
## Call:
## lda(data, group)
## 
## Prior probabilities of groups:
##     setosa versicolor  virginica 
##  0.3333333  0.3333333  0.3333333 
## 
## Group means:
##            Petal.Length Sepal.Length
## setosa            1.462        5.006
## versicolor        4.260        5.936
## virginica         5.552        6.588
## 
## Coefficients of linear discriminants:
##                    LD1        LD2
## Petal.Length  3.427642 -0.9282016
## Sepal.Length -1.658896  2.4617343
## 
## Proportion of trace:
##    LD1    LD2 
## 0.9987 0.0013
data <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Petal.Width, Sepal.Length)

group <- iris %>%
    pull(Species)

lda(data, group)
## Call:
## lda(data, group)
## 
## Prior probabilities of groups:
##     setosa versicolor  virginica 
##  0.3333333  0.3333333  0.3333333 
## 
## Group means:
##            Petal.Width Sepal.Length
## setosa           0.246        5.006
## versicolor       1.326        5.936
## virginica        2.026        6.588
## 
## Coefficients of linear discriminants:
##                      LD1       LD2
## Petal.Width   4.91087360 -1.848948
## Sepal.Length -0.02715312  2.085884
## 
## Proportion of trace:
##    LD1    LD2 
## 0.9999 0.0001
data <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Petal.Width, Sepal.Width)

group <- iris %>%
    pull(Species)

lda(data, group)
## Call:
## lda(data, group)
## 
## Prior probabilities of groups:
##     setosa versicolor  virginica 
##  0.3333333  0.3333333  0.3333333 
## 
## Group means:
##            Petal.Width Sepal.Width
## setosa           0.246       3.428
## versicolor       1.326       2.770
## virginica        2.026       2.974
##

## Petal.Width  5.477136 0.8169648
## Sepal.Width -1.986964 2.6800746

## Proportion of trace:

## 0.9884 0.0116
data <- iris %>%
    dplyr::select(Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width)

group <- iris %>%
    pull(Species)

lda(data, group)
## Call:
## lda(data, group)

##  0.3333333  0.3333333  0.3333333

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Petal.Width, y = Sepal.Length, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Petal Width", y = "Sepal Length") + theme_bw()

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Width, color = Species)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Edgar Anderson's Iris Data",
    x = " Sepal Length", y = "Petal Width") + theme_bw()

Question 4

I used the LDA and scatter plot visualizations to evaluate which two measurements gave the best predictions. While the data supports Edgar Anderson’s predictions, I would not be confident in the data based solely off LDA and plots for only petal and sepal sizes and would need further evidence to prove that they are separate species that created a hybrid.

Part II: Geographical and temporal data

Question 1

This is a species of stag beetle known scientifically as Platycerus oregonensis found mostly in Oregon at about 50 latitude and 120 longitude.

Platycerus <- get.gbif("Platycerus", "oregonensis")

ggplot(world.map, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", colour = "grey50") + coord_quickmap() + geom_point(data = Platycerus,                                                                                   mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5) +labs(title = "Location of Platycerus oregonensis")

ggplot(world.map, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", colour = "grey50") + coord_quickmap() + geom_point(data = Platycerus,                                                                                   mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5)+coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-160, -80), ylim = c(25,75))+labs(title = "Location of Platycerus oregonensis Zoomed In")

## Question 2

us_iris_gps <- iris_gps %>% filter(lat > 25, lat <= 49, lon > -130, lon <=-67)

ggplot(us.map, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", colour = "grey50") + coord_quickmap() + geom_point(data = us_iris_gps, mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5) + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-125,-67), ylim = c(20,50))

Question 3

us_north_east_iris_gps <- us_iris_gps %>% filter(lat > 40, lat <= 47.5, lon > -85, lon <= -67)

ggplot(us.map, aes(long, lat, group = group)) + geom_polygon(fill = "white", colour = "grey50") + coord_quickmap() + geom_point(data = us_north_east_iris_gps, mapping = aes(x = lon, y = lat, group = species, color = species), size = 0.5) + coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-85,-67), ylim = c(40,48))

Question 4

Iris setosa is the least distributed in the Northeast region of the U.S, iris versicolor covers mostly the eastern side of the Northeast region while Iris virginica covers mostly the western side. Both iris veriscolor and iris virginica overlap within the middle region of the Northeast, and iris setosa overlaps a small area in the east-most side with iris versicolor. This tells us that iris setosa lives separately from the other two species, and iris versicolor and iris virginica have overlapping areas, but have certain zones that they live in. If the three species share similar distribution in the Northeast region of the U.S, the species delineation may be based off genetic differences and/or temporal differences and possibly supports the prediction of iris versicolor being a hybrid.

Question 5

Using only geographical and temporal data to evaluate reproductive isolation is problematic because it does not account for all other possible barriers if the data were to overlap. For example, iris versicolor and iris virginica overlap in both geographical distribution and times of flowering, which does not distinguish them from each other as species. Other factors such as behavior, mechanical parts of the flowers, and gametic compatibility are not accounted for when evaluating reproductive isolation based solely off geographical and temporal data.

temporal_iris_gps <- iris_gps %>% dplyr::select(species,month) %>% mutate(month_name = factor(month, levels = 1:12, labels = c("Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun", "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"))) %>% drop_na(month)


ggplot(data = temporal_iris_gps, aes(x = month_name, group = species, fill = species)) + geom_bar(position = "dodge", binwidth = 1) + facet_wrap(~species, nrow = 3) + labs(x = "month", y = "density", title = "Flowering Times of the Iris species") +theme_minimal()
## Warning in geom_bar(position = "dodge", binwidth = 1): Ignoring unknown
## parameters: `binwidth`

Part III: Chromosomal data

Question 1

iris_chrom_clean <- iris_chrom %>% mutate(sporophytic = str_extract(sporophytic, "\\d+$",), sporophytic = as.numeric(sporophytic)) %>% drop_na(sporophytic)
                                                   
iris_chrom_mode <- iris_chrom_clean %>% count(species, sporophytic, sort = TRUE) %>% group_by(species) %>% slice_max(n, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>% dplyr::select(species, mode_sporophytic = sporophytic)

head(iris_chrom_mode, 20)
## # A tibble: 20 × 2
## # Groups:   species [20]
##    species        mode_sporophytic
##    <chr>                     <dbl>
##  1 acutiloba                    20
##  2 afghanica                    22
##  3 aitchisonii                  34
##  4 alberti                      24
##  5 albicans                     44
##  6 albomarginata                50
##  7 anguifuga                    22
##  8 antilibanotica               20
##  9 aphylla                      48
## 10 arenaria                     22
## 11 assadiana                    20
## 12 atrofusca                    20
## 13 atropatana                   18
## 14 atropurpurea                 20
## 15 aucheri                      24
## 16 auranitica                   20
## 17 barnumiae                    20
## 18 basaltica                    20
## 19 bicapitata                   40
## 20 biglumis                     40

Question 2

ggplot(iris_chrom_clean, aes(x = sporophytic)) + geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),binwidth = 1,) +labs(title = "Sporophytic Counts in Iris Species", x = "Sporophytic Counts", y = "Density") + theme_minimal()

Question 3

The mode value for each species is 38 for iris setosa, 108 for iris versicolor, and 70 for iris virginica. The data confirms the hypothesis that iris versicolor is a hybrid of iris setosa and iris virginica since the chromosome mode numbers of iris setosa and iris virginica combined make up the chromosome count for iris versicolor, indicating that hybridization occurred.

Part IV: Producing a scientific argument

Question 1

The morphological data, geographical data, and temporal data between the 3 potential species showed that iris setosa had distinct features, while iris versicolor and iris virginica overlapped with one another slightly, but were distinct enough to be considered potentially different. The chromosomal data helped distinguished these 2 species by showing the ploidy of iris versicolor and iris virginica were different from each other. Iris versicolor had 108 counts, and iris setosa had 38 and iris virginica had 70, which provide the evidence of possible allopolyploidy in iris versicolor. The most compelling piece of data was the chromosomal data since it showed potentially polyploidy in iris versicolor that may have resulted from hybridization from the other 2 species. The original designations from Anderson were correct. The geographic, temporal, and chromosomal data added onto his original data since they provided additional information that supported Anderson’s findings that solely focused on morphological differences. Based on this, these organisms represent 3 different species since the ploidy of each species is different and they are mostly geographically isolated from one another.
